title: TLS Handshake
description: The negotiation process that establishes a TLS session — agreeing on cipher suite, exchanging keys, and authenticating the server.
fullExplanation: The TLS handshake establishes a secure channel before any application data is sent. TLS 1.3 reduced this to 1 RTT (from 2 RTT in TLS 1.2) by sending the key share in the first message. The handshake authenticates the server (via certificate), negotiates cipher suite, and derives session keys using ECDHE key exchange. All subsequent data is encrypted with the negotiated symmetric key.

tls13Steps.name: ClientHello
tls13Steps.description: Supported TLS versions, cipher suites, key share (X25519 public key), random nonce, SNI (server name)

tls13Steps.name: ServerHello
tls13Steps.description: Chosen cipher suite, server's key share (X25519 public key), random nonce

tls13Steps.name: CertificateVerify
tls13Steps.description: Signature over handshake transcript — proves server owns private key

tls13Steps.name: Server Finished
tls13Steps.description: HMAC over entire handshake — integrity check

tls13Steps.name: Client Finished
tls13Steps.description: Client verifies cert, sends Finished MAC. Keys derived. 1 RTT total.

tls13Steps.name: Application Data
tls13Steps.description: Encrypted data flows using negotiated keys

keyDerivation.description: TLS 1.3 uses HKDF (HMAC-based Key Derivation Function) to derive multiple keys
keys: Handshake traffic keys (encrypt handshake messages after ServerHello)

keyFacts: 0-RTT: client sends data in first message using resumption key — replay attack risk
SNI (Server Name Indication): client sends hostname in ClientHello — allows virtual hosting
ESNI/ECH: encrypts SNI to prevent ISP/firewall from seeing which site you're visiting
ALPN (Application-Layer Protocol Negotiation): negotiate HTTP/2 vs HTTP/1.1 in handshake
Session tickets: server sends encrypted session state to client for fast resumption
Certificate pinning: client only accepts specific certificate or public key
